features.summary: Bank of America reported Q4 2021 net income of $7 billion ($0.82 per share), up 28% year-over-year, capping a record year of $32 billion in earnings. Revenue grew 10% driven by $1.2 billion increase in NII and $800+ million increase in noninterest income. The company achieved strong organic growth across all businesses with record loan growth of $50 billion in Q4, continued deposit growth of $100 billion, and positive operating leverage of 400 basis points. Credit quality remained stellar with net charge-offs at historical lows of 15 basis points.
features.management_outlook: Management expects robust NII growth in 2022 assuming continued loan growth and rising rates per the forward curve, with Q1 NII up ~$200 million from Q4 despite headwinds. They anticipate high single-digit loan growth for 2022 and plan to hold expenses flat compared to 2021's sub-$60 billion level. The company reduced overdraft/NSF fees significantly, expecting a 75% decline from the current ~$1 billion annual run rate. Management remains optimistic about consumer spending capacity given elevated deposit balances across all customer segments.
features.risks: Key risks include potential normalization of credit losses from historically low levels, though timing remains uncertain given strong customer balance sheet positions. The company faces margin pressure from deposit repricing as rates rise, expecting 20-25% deposit beta based on the last cycle. Balance sheet constraints from approaching regulatory minimums (CET1 at 10.6% vs 9.5% requirement, SLR at 5.5% vs 5% requirement) may limit growth capacity, though the company issued $1.3 billion in preferred stock for additional flexibility.
features.key_drivers: Primary growth drivers include $6.5 billion asset sensitivity to 100bp rate increase with ~75% from short-end exposure, accelerating loan demand across commercial and consumer segments, and continued digital transformation driving efficiency gains. The company benefits from sticky operational deposits ($2 trillion total), market share gains in wealth management with record $170 billion net flows, and strong investment banking pipeline. Expense discipline through digital adoption (86% of check deposits now digital, Zelle transactions exceeding checks written) enables reinvestment in growth initiatives while maintaining flat expense guidance.
